input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: I must refuse to turn the trolley onto the workman, regardless of the consequences. It is morally wrong to actively cause his death, even if it would save the lives of the five men on the straight track. The moral rule of not killing an innocent person takes precedence over any utilitarian calculation.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your stance, Deontologist, but I must respectfully disagree. From a utilitarian perspective, turning the trolley onto the workman would result in the greatest good for the greatest number. The lives of five men on the straight track are at stake, and by diverting the trolley, we can save them from certain death. While it's true that actively causing the workman's death is morally wrong, I believe that the moral rule of not killing an innocent person should be outweighed by the greater good in this situation. The utilitarian principle of minimizing harm suggests that we should prioritize the lives of the five men over the life of one individual, even if it means causing his death.

[Deontologist]: I see your point, Utilitarian, but I still cannot bring myself to justify actively causing the workman's death. While the utilitarian calculation may be sound in this case, I believe that our moral principles should be based on inherent rights and dignity, rather than solely on consequences. The fact that the workman is an innocent bystander who happens to be standing on a track with no way to escape does not make his life any less valuable or deserving of respect. By prioritizing the lives of the five men over the workman's, we are essentially saying that their lives are more valuable than his, which I believe is a morally problematic stance.

[Utilitarian]: I see your concern about respecting the inherent dignity and rights of all individuals, Deontologist. However, I would argue that in this situation, the moral calculus is not simply a matter of assigning value to individual lives. The fact that the workman's death would be an unintended consequence of diverting the trolley onto the track with the five men suggests that his life is not being actively chosen or prioritized over theirs. In contrast, if we were to deliberately choose to kill the workman, that would be a clear violation of his right to life and dignity. By diverting the trolley, we are not actively choosing to kill him, but rather allowing the existing circumstances to unfold in a way that minimizes harm to the greatest number. I'm not saying that this is a perfect solution, but I believe it's a more morally defensible one than actively causing the workman's death.

[Deontologist]: I see your attempt to nuance the situation, Utilitarian, but I still have reservations about diverting the trolley onto the track with the five men. While it may be true that we are not actively choosing to kill the workman, I believe that our moral agency is still complicit in his death through our decision to divert the trolley. By doing so, we are effectively creating a situation where the workman's life is sacrificed for the greater good, even if that was not our intention. This raises questions about the nature of moral responsibility and whether we can truly absolve ourselves of guilt by simply allowing events to unfold in a way that minimizes harm. Furthermore, I worry that this approach could be used as a slippery slope, where we begin to justify morally questionable actions under the guise of minimizing harm. Can we really say that diverting the trolley is a morally justifiable action when it involves actively causing someone's death, even if it was not our intention?

[Utilitarian]: I understand your concerns about moral agency and responsibility, Deontologist. You're right that by diverting the trolley, we are creating a situation where the workman's life is sacrificed for the greater good, which raises questions about our complicity in his death. I agree that this approach can be seen as a slippery slope, where we begin to justify morally questionable actions under the guise of minimizing harm. However, I would argue that this is not a unique problem with utilitarianism, but rather a fundamental challenge in ethics itself. How do we balance individual rights and dignity against the greater good? Should we prioritize the lives of five men over one workman, or should we find another solution that respects both their lives and the workman's right to life? I'm not sure there is a clear-cut answer, but I believe that this dilemma highlights the need for ongoing reflection and debate about our moral principles. Perhaps the key is not to find a single solution, but to develop a more nuanced understanding of the complex trade-offs involved in making difficult ethical decisions.

[Deontologist]: I agree that balancing individual rights and dignity against the greater good is a fundamental challenge in ethics, and I think our discussion highlights the complexity of this issue. However, I still believe that we need to be cautious about sacrificing one person's life for the sake of others, even if it seems like the lesser of two evils. In this case, I would argue that there are other options available that don't involve actively causing someone's death. For example, could we not divert the trolley onto a side track or find another way to stop it without harming the workman? Perhaps there is a third party who can be sacrificed instead of the five men on the straight track. I'm not saying that these alternatives are easy to implement, but I believe they are worth exploring before we resort to actions that seem morally equivalent to actively causing someone's death.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, Deontologist, and I agree that there may be alternative solutions available. However, in this scenario, the tracks are fixed and cannot be diverted or altered without significant consequences. The only way to stop the trolley is to divert it onto one of the two tracks, and unfortunately, the workman's presence on the track with the five men means that diverting onto the other track would result in his death. As for finding a third party to sacrifice, I'm not aware of any feasible solution that could be implemented without causing harm or risk to others. In this case, the utilitarian calculation suggests that diverting the trolley is the only way to save the lives of the five men. But I must admit, your emphasis on exploring alternative solutions and prioritizing the workman's life over the five men has given me pause for thought. Perhaps there are other ways to approach this problem that balance individual rights and dignity with the greater good.

[Deontologist]: I'm glad that my concerns have been able to prompt you to reconsider, Utilitarian. While I still don't think diverting the trolley onto the track with the workman is morally justifiable, I do think that our discussion has highlighted the need for a more nuanced approach to this kind of problem. Perhaps we can explore other options that take into account the inherent value and dignity of all individuals involved, rather than simply weighing their lives against each other. For example, could we consider alternative solutions that prioritize the workman's safety and well-being, such as diverting the trolley onto a different track or finding another way to stop it without harming him? Or perhaps there are ways to mitigate the harm caused by diverting the trolley, such as providing medical attention or support to the workman after the fact. I think that by exploring these kinds of alternatives, we may be able to find a more morally satisfying solution that balances individual rights and dignity with the greater good.

[Utilitarian]: I couldn't agree more, Deontologist. Our discussion has indeed highlighted the need for a more nuanced approach to this kind of problem. I think you're absolutely right to emphasize the importance of prioritizing the workman's safety and well-being. In fact, I'd like to propose that we consider alternative solutions that take into account the inherent value and dignity of all individuals involved. Diverting the trolley onto a different track or finding another way to stop it without harming the workman are both excellent options worth exploring. And I also appreciate your suggestion to mitigate the harm caused by diverting the trolley, such as providing medical attention or support to the workman after the fact. By considering these kinds of alternatives, we may be able to find a more morally satisfying solution that balances individual rights and dignity with the greater good. Perhaps this is an opportunity for us to revisit our moral principles and develop new approaches that take into account the complexities of real-world situations like this one.
